data.title: Goldman Focuses on 'Sanctions-Driven Frictions' for Iranian, Russian Oil — OPIS
data.description:
Goldman Sachs commodity analysts on Wednesday offered their take on what tougher U.S. sanctions on Russia and President Trump's Tuesday decision to restore "maximum pressure" on Iran could mean for Brent crude oil prices.

The investment bank's report attributed Tuesday's $2/bbl intraday jump in Brent prices to the White House announcement that it would reimpose strict sanctions against Iran to limit the country's ability to acquire a nuclear weapon. Part of the strategy called for limiting Iranian oil shipments to other countries, excluding China, to zero.

Goldman's analysts also said higher Russian and Iranian liquids supply of late equates to an increase of only about 200,000 b/d and focused on "sanctions-driven frictions" for oil from both countries.

The report said Russian oil in on-the-water storage has risen by about 17 million bbl since former President Joe Biden announced stepped-up sanctions on Jan. 10.

The bank said it believes Russia could have as much as 50 million bbl in waterborne storage in the first half of 2025. It added that would effectively keep oil away from refiners and blenders and boost prices by about $2/bbl.Floating storage is also important for Iran, the analysts said, calculating that it has risen by 14 million bbl since the start of the year, the highest level in 14 months.

The bank's outlook for Brent prices includes just a 400,000 b/d decline in Iranian production this year, but it warned that enforcement of the stricter sanctions could drop the country's output by 1 million b/d. That, along with a potential 6% decline in Guyananese production due to planned first-quarter maintenance, could boost prices to the high $80s/bbl by May, it said.

Goldman also estimated that refined product demand in India has risen by 4% so far this year and said colder weather in Asia and the U.S. could raise heating oil demand by 100,000 b/d in the next two weeks.

The bank's report also blamed positioning for the February downturn, saying total length for the managed money category in futures recently dropped by about 47 million bbl.

Further, Goldman said it believes Chinese demand has dropped by about 100,000 b/d since Jan. 1 and it expects the country's consumption will remain flat through the current year.

The report paid less attention to Trump's tariff moves, but reiterated several tactical trades it discussed in January for those who believe a sustained U.S. tariff on Canadian crude oil is unlikely.

Goldman suggested betting on a narrowing discount between Western Canadian Select prices in Hardisty, Alberta, and Houston. It said the spread has so far delivered a profit of 49cts/bbl.

A second trading strategy, playing the "Canadian fade," would involve shorting New York Harbor heating oil and being long in European gasoil. That play, however, showed a paper loss of about 57cts/bbl late Tuesday.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

Reporting by Tom Kloza, tkloza@opisnet.com; Editing by Jeff Barber, jbarber@opisnet.com

data.title: Canada Offers to Address U.S. Priority Trade Issues, Except Dairy — Market Talk
data.description:
1811 ET - Canadian Trade Minister Mary Ng says Ottawa is ready to enter talks with Trump administration officials on "priority" bilateral trade issues with a goal of avoiding a 25% tariff in early March. Ng provides no concrete examples on what the Liberal government might do, during an interview with Canada's CTV News. Ng, though, says Ottawa won't make concessions on agriculture, especially measures aimed at protecting Canada's dairy producers. Canada's system of protecting dairy farmers was a point of attack for President Trump's nominee for Commerce Secretary, Howard Lutnick, during a confirmation hearing last week. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1808 ET - Ford CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts that the company believes President Trump and his administration, as well as other congressional leaders, are committed to strengthening the U.S. auto industry. "That is certainly our expectation, and we look forward to working with our leaders to make sure that that becomes a reality, because they understand and appreciate how vital our industry is to jobs, the economy, our national security and the communities across our country," he says. Shares fall 5.3% after the company says it expects profitability to shrink in 2025. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1715 ET - Despite a tough farm economy in 2024, Corteva expects improvements this year. "We acknowledge the fluid macro environment but still see ag market fundamentals improving - specifically, we see continued record demand for core crops and recovering grain prices and farm margins," CEO Chuck Magro said in a statement. Shares of the agriculture company ticked down 6% during post-market trading after it said sales rose 7% to about $4 billion in its latest quarter, slightly below analyst expectations for the period. Corteva, one of the largest sellers of crop seeds and chemicals in the world, and other agriculture companies faced a tough agriculture market this past year because of declining grain prices and rising costs. Corteva said sales volumes for its pesticides and seeds both were up double digits for the quarter that ended Dec. 31 compared with the prior year as prices for its products ticked down. (patrick.thomas@wsj.com)

1607 ET - U.S. stocks rise as political turbulence takes a step back and indicators point to a cooling economy that could allow the Fed to cut interest rates down the road. Information technology is the best performing S&P 500 sector, up nearly 2%. Server-maker Super Micro Computer rises 8% on advances of an AI data center solution that uses Nvidia's chips. Nvidia, in turn, rises 5%. The DJIA gains 317 points, or nearly 0.7%, to 44873. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq are slightly higher, at 6061 and 19692, respectively. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1559 ET - Uber Technologies' latest results gave a lot of details the market has yet to digest, such as another frequency growth deceleration in membership gains, with the 1Q outlook calling for more of the same, Benchmark analysts say in a research note. The ride sharing app's incremental margins also took a 1% step down from where they had been running as it continues to invest in growth initiatives, the analysts say. But when it comes to the autonomous vehicle race, Uber has a real opportunity to showcase their value in Austin and Atlanta this year, with full scaled offerings and demand chain management starting in March, the analysts add. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1558 ET - Treasury yields fall again, with the 10-year dropping below 4.5% for the first time since Dec. 18, as relatively soft economic indicators retake center stage and tariff-related inflation fears ebb. ADP jobs report was stronger than expected, but the trade deficit was wider than forecast and services PMI surprisingly fell. Weekly jobless claims tomorrow are expected to indicate increasing layoffs and slowing job creation is forecast for Friday's payrolls report. The 10-year falls 0.090 percentage point to 4.421% and the two-year declines 0.030 to 4.183% (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1552 ET - Uber Technologies's approach to the autonomous vehicles market isn't convincing investors yet, Melius Research analysts say in a research note. The ride-sharing app provided a little more clarity on its strategy regarding AVs on a long-term basis, but that doesn't seem to be enough to change the conversation and therefore created volatility, the analysts say. "With AV development still in early days, it's hard to tell how Uber ultimately fits in," the analysts say. "There is merit to Uber driving utilization and demand, but as Waymo, Tesla, etc. introduce their own platforms, they will gather data and build knowledge around demand patterns as well," the analysts add. Shares fall 7.6% to $64.46. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1528 ET - The U.S. poultry industry faces a trade-off between technological advances and operational constraints, Santander's Guilherme Palhares and Laura Hirata write. While genetic advances have resulted in heavier chickens, the new lineages are seeing an imbalance in mating ratios and specific diets for roosters, they say. Palhares and Hirata see JBS, which controls Pilgrim's Pride, as better positioned than its competitors to get around the problem. JBS "has not been affected by genetic disruptions, as it works with a wider range of lineages, reducing operational risks," they write. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1522 ET - Corporate spreads are tight, but could get tighter, Angel Oak's Clayton Triick says. He likes high-yield U.S. corporate credit because he sees yields as likely to lower and prices to increase. "The quality of the high-yield bond market has improved significantly," he says. Triick adds that the high-yield landscape now has more bonds rated BB and less rated C, reflecting shrinking risk in the asset class. "So there is room for high yield spreads to go a little bit tighter from here." That said, Triick sees more room for gains in mortgage-backed securities than in corporate bonds. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1516 ET - The Fed probably wants short-term yields below 4% with longer-term ones well above it, says Clayton Triick, head of portfolio management of public strategies at Angel Oak. In Wall Street parlance, policymakers would like the yield curve to steepen. "A steeper curve [is] good for growth, for debt growth, good for banks, and good for the economy." It would also be good for bonds in the front-end of the yield curve, which are mostly above 4%. Their prices would rise if yields fell. "I think there's a good chance that bonds could can do quite well" as the curve steepens, Triick says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1500 ET - Fed governor Michelle Bowman notes that Treasury-market liquidity has been a steady source of concern for years, and suggests that regulatory reforms for banks could ease the problem. Speaking at a banking conference in Topeka, Kan., Bowman notes that when bank balance sheets expand, regulatory leverage ratios can become a binding constraint, even if the bank is investing in safe assets. "When constrained in this way, bank-affiliated primary dealers may pull back on market intermediation activities," Bowman says. "Where we can take proactive regulatory measures to ensure that primary dealers have adequate balance sheet capacity to intermediate Treasury markets, we should do so." She suggests amending the leverage ratio and G-SIB surcharge regulations for big U.S. banks could help. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1458 ET - Investors grew worried about inflation during the trade war flare-up, but the U.S. housing market is likely to counter price pressures elsewhere, Angel Oak's Clayton Triick says. He expects new-home supply to increase, cooling prices and helping keep the Fed on track to cut rates. Housing market dynamics also make investing in mortgages an attractive option for fixed-income investors. Triick is particularly bullish on higher-yielding mortgages not backed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. "You are getting elevated yields and the fundamentals of housing are still really strong," he says. A drop on house prices could increase defaults, but Triick says it is unlikely to happen. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1812 ET - Comet Ridge's signal that a final investment decision on its Mahalo natural-gas project in Australia won't happen this year contributes to Bell Potter cutting its price target by 19% to A$0.21/share. Still, analyst Stuart Howe remains bullish about Comet Ridge, highlighting that Mahalo is one of the few development-ready projects capable of delivering near-term gas supply to eastern Australia. Comet Ridge expects preliminary design and engineering work at Mahalo to continue through this year. Only when this is completed will it make a final investment decision, or FID, with partner Santos. "We expect Comet Ridge's majority equity positions in the Mahalo projects and a strong gas market outlook to provide support for development funding across debt, equity and gas prepayments," Bell Potter says. "However, we also acknowledge risks to FID timing." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1811 ET - Canadian Trade Minister Mary Ng says Ottawa is ready to enter talks with Trump administration officials on "priority" bilateral trade issues with a goal of avoiding a 25% tariff in early March. Ng provides no concrete examples on what the Liberal government might do, during an interview with Canada's CTV News. Ng, though, says Ottawa won't make concessions on agriculture, especially measures aimed at protecting Canada's dairy producers. Canada's system of protecting dairy farmers was a point of attack for President Trump's nominee for Commerce Secretary, Howard Lutnick, during a confirmation hearing last week. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1744 ET - Beach Energy's interim dividend falls short of market expectations, with Barrenjoey noting the payout doesn't align with the company's distribution policy. Beach declared an interim dividend of 3 Australian cents a share. While that was up 50% on last year it fell short of the 4 cents anticipated by the market. Analyst Dale Koenders points out that Beach reported free cash flow of A$431 million in 1H. "But this free cash flow then also implies a payout ratio of only 16%, below policy of 40-50%," Koenders says in his initial response to the result. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1540 ET - Oil futures fall as the EIA reports a bigger-then-expected 8.7 million barrel increase in U.S. crude inventories. "Tariff developments have buffeted the entire energy complex with potential additional restraints on Iranian crude exports adding to geopolitical uncertainties," Ritterbusch says in a note. "But with the tariffs on Canada and Mexico likely pushed to the sidelines for a couple of weeks, the oil market will be able to zero in more clearly on underlying fundamentals." U.S. gasoline stocks extended their buildup to 12 weeks, while distillate stocks fell by a hefty 5.5 million barrels. "We continue to view gasoline as the weak leg in the complex," Ritterbusch adds. WTI settles down 2.3% at $71.03 a barrel, and Brent falls 2.1% to $74.61. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1517 ET - U.S. natural gas futures pick up ahead of tomorrow's weekly inventory report that's expected to leave the storage deficit against the five-year average largely intact. Analysts in a Wall Street Journal survey predict a withdrawal of 170 Bcf to 2,401 Bcf, which would be 107 Bcf below the five-year average and 204 Bcf less than a year ago. Temperature forecasts and supply and demand fundamentals were little changed, and LNG feedgas nominations "look to be in good shape with a 15.1 Bcf/d flow," Gelber & Associates says in a note. The Nymex front month settles up 3.3% at $3.360/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1445 ET - A third large weekly inventory draw leaves U.S. distillate stocks 12% below their five-year average as a colder-than-expected January drove extra demand for heating fuel. The inventory decline "is not too surprising given where refineries are operating, still below 85% of operable capacity, but that is something that creates near-term price risk for diesel," says Matt Muenster, chief economist at transport technology firm Breakthrough. Monday's pause in threatened tariffs on Canadian oil took some pressure off prices, and although the cost would likely be shared among Canadian oil producers, U.S. refiners and consumers, "if they had stayed, that ultimately was going to get passed to transportation pretty quickly," he adds. Also offsetting the price impact of lower diesel stocks is the seasonal build in crude oil inventories, Muenster says. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1333 ET - Global sustainable bond issuance is expected to remain steady in 2025, at about $1 trillion, according to S&P Global Ratings in a report. The agency says "with green bonds and supranational entities in the lead, the sustainable bond market seems to have returned to its roots." Corporate green bond issuance is now well established, S&P says. However, issuance fell in 2024 to about 11% of the global total, from 13% in 2023. More issuance in low-income and emerging economies is expected to address the climate finance gap, S&P says, but adds "we see a risk that macroeconomic and political factors could stifle further growth." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1223 ET - Oil prices slide 2% despite U.S. President Trump reinstating maximum pressure on Iran, as rising U.S. crude inventories and fears of a trade war raise demand concerns. Brent crude is down 1.9% to $74.75 a barrel, while WTI falls 2.1% to $71.21 a barrel. The latest data from the Energy Information Administration show crude stockpiles rose by 8.7 million barrels last week, against expectations of a 1.3-million-barrel rise. Prices are also pressured by concerns that a trade spat between the U.S. and China could hurt global growth and raise inflationary pressures. "The oil market is now caught between increasing fears that an escalating trade war will damage global oil demand growth...and possible sudden disruption of Iranian oil exports," says SEB's chief commodities analyst Bjarne Schieldrop in a note. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1055 ET - The European Union's need to rapidly replenish dwindling gas reserves is expected to boost imports of liquefied natural gas by more than 20% this year, Kpler analysts say. According to the data provider, imports are estimated to increase to 102.3 million tons from 84.4 million tons in 2024. "LNG will play a crucial role in Europe's efforts to offset the loss of Russian gas supply via Ukraine, particularly during the restocking season, given that underground storage levels are expected to end this winter significantly lower than in the past two years," the analysts say. In January, imports to the bloc's 27 members rose to 8.35 million tons, up from 7.30 million tons the previous month due to colder temperatures and lower wind power generation. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1047 ET - Shares of grain merchant and oilseed processor Bunge were down about 6% after the company reported a lower quarterly profit that missed analyst expectations because of supply gluts for crops and the meal and oil they are processed into. Bunge CEO told analysts on a call that the future for the agriculture market is hard to predict given the Trump Administration's threats of trade wars and what will happen to the U.S.' biofuels policies that drive the industry. "We definitely are in an environment has less visibility than the normal with the trade disruptions and some of the uncertainty around U.S. biofuels," Heckman said. He added that the company's proposed acquisition of rival trader Viterra will help it weather any trade fights by increasing the company's ability to source crops from around the world. (patrick.thomas@wsj.com)

1014 ET - Equinor cuts capital expenditure as expected, but shareholder distributions are disappointing, Jefferies analysts Giacomo Romeo and Kai Ye Loh write. Updated guidance reflects consensus expectations in terms of the capex cut, due to lower renewables spending. However, 2025 distributions are lower than expected and the outlook for 2026 distributions remains unclear, they say. The 2025 dividend and buyback of $9 billion is below consensus at $10 billion, while Equinor has scrapped its 2026 buyback guidance. Quarterly earnings beat consensus on net income but are relatively inline on the operating line and on cash flow from operations. Shares trade 3.5% lower at 268.20 Norwegian kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0925 ET - Shifting weather forecasts continue to buffet U.S. natural gas futures, which are adding modestly to yesterday's losses. "Winter is not yet over," EBW Analytics says in a note. "The near term may see mild weather and bearish risks for Thursday's EIA report-but a subsequent bounce is probable." Colder temperatures ahead coupled with a storage deficit are bullish, while medium-term, higher production should be a counterweight, the firm adds. Nymex natural gas is off 0.7% at $3.229/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Computershare Price Target Raised 12% to A$31.10/Share by Morgan Stanley
Comet Ridge Keeps Bull Onside Despite Later Mahalo FID — Market Talk
data.description: Comet Ridge's signal that a final investment decision on its Mahalo natural-gas project in Australia won't happen this year contributes to Bell Potter cutting its price target by 19% to A$0.21/share. Still, analyst Stuart Howe remains bullish about Comet Ridge, highlighting that Mahalo is one of the few development-ready projects capable of delivering near-term gas supply to eastern Australia. Comet Ridge expects preliminary design and engineering work at Mahalo to continue through this year. Only when this is completed will it make a final investment decision, or FID, with partner Santos. "We expect Comet Ridge's majority equity positions in the Mahalo projects and a strong gas market outlook to provide support for development funding across debt, equity and gas prepayments," Bell Potter says. "However, we also acknowledge risks to FID timing." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: CFO of Office of Personnel Management is resigning, ABC News reports
data.description:
Erica Roach, the chief financial officer of the federal government's Office of Personnel Management, is resigning after being pushed out of the role, an ABC News reporter said on X, citing sources familiar with the move.

Aides to billionaire Elon Musk recently took control of the OPM - the U.S. government human resources agency - as part of the Trump administration's Department of Government Efficiency initiative.

Since taking office, President Donald Trump has embarked on a massive government makeover, firing and sidelining hundreds of civil servants in his first steps toward downsizing the bureaucracy and installing more loyalists.

Roach did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

data.title: RentFi Launching, Making Real Estate Investment Available to Everyone
data.description:
ROAD TOWN, BVI, Feb. 05, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — RentFi has successfully launched its blockchain-based real estate investment platform, making property investment as simple as buying a token. Through its $RENT token, now actively trading, investors worldwide can earn rental income without the traditional hurdles of property ownership.

"Real estate investment has always been profitable but often unreachable for most people. We've changed that by making it as easy as buying any other digital asset," explains the RentFi Foundation. "Anyone with a smartphone can now invest in real estate and earn rental income."

RentFi's platform transforms how people invest in property through several key innovations:

The platform distributes rental income in two ways: 50% goes directly to token holders as regular passive income, while the other 50% is used for token buybacks and burns, helping increase token value over time.

Built on the Solana blockchain, RentFi ensures that transactions are fast and affordable. The platform has set a maximum supply of 100 million tokens, and implementing a deflationary system that supports long-term value growth.

"Traditional real estate investment typically requires large down payments, complex paperwork, and ongoing property management," says the RentFi Foundation. "Our platform eliminates these barriers. Token holders can start earning rental income without dealing with tenants, maintenance, or legal complexities."

The project's innovative approach provides several benefits for investors:

  • Access to a diverse property portfolio, reducing the risks typically associated with single-property investments
  • Regular rental income distributed automatically to token holders
  • No property management responsibilities
  • Easy entry and exit through token trading
  • Potential for token value appreciation through systematic buybacks

RentFi marks a significant step forward in making real estate investment accessible to everyone. Through its global portfolio, the platform combines the stability of property investment with the convenience of digital assets, creating new opportunities for both experienced investors and newcomers to the real estate market.

Investors interested in participating can now purchase $RENT tokens through major cryptocurrency exchanges. For more information about RentFi and its innovative approach to real estate investment, visit rentfi.io or follow on X: @RentFi_io

About RentFi

RentFi Limited, the first-ever Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT) on blockchain, is revolutionizing property investment by making it accessible to everyone. By combining traditional real estate with blockchain technology, RentFi creates new opportunities for global investors to earn rental income without the complexities of direct property ownership.

data.title: Seibu Holdings Repurchases 2.2 Million Shares in January
data.description:
Seibu Holdings repurchased 2.2 million shares for a total of 7.11 billion yen in January, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The company's buyback program, approved in December 2024, allows for the repurchase of up to 28 million shares, with a total value of up to 70 billion yen.

data.title: Mattel Is Maintained at Buy by DA Davidson
data.description: (23:14 GMT) Mattel Price Target Raised to $30.00/Share From $27.00 by DA Davidson

data.title: Aflac's fourth-quarter profit misses on Japan unit weakness
data.description:
Aflac reported a fourth-quarter profit on Wednesday that missed Wall Street estimates, hurt by weakness in the health and life insurer's Japan unit.

The company provides accident and pet insurance plans through its units in the U.S. and Japan. It also offers supplemental insurance for out-of-pocket expenses not typically covered by major medical insurers.

On an adjusted basis, Aflac earned $1.56 per share, compared with the average analyst estimate of $1.62, according to data compiled by LSEG.

The company's Japan unit reported adjusted sales of 359.9 billion yen ($2.36 billion), below estimates of $2.62 billion.

The insurer reported revenue of $5.4 billion, missing estimates of $4.16 billion.

($1 = 152.5200 yen)

data.title: News Corp Earnings Rise, Buoyed by Dow Jones and Digital Real Estate Segments — WSJ
data.description:
By Jeffrey A. Trachtenberg

News Corp's revenue and profit increased in its fiscal second quarter, reflecting gains at its Dow Jones, digital real estate services and book publishing segments.

Quarterly revenue at the company, whose properties include The Wall Street Journal and HarperCollins Publishers, increased 4.8% to $2.24 billion. Analysts had expected revenue of $2.18 billion.

News Corp earned $215 million, or 40 cents per share, in the quarter. Analysts had expected earnings of 30 cents per share.

A year ago, the company reported earnings of $156 million, or 28 cents per share.

Class A shares fell 2% in after-market trading. The stock has risen 19% over the past 12 months.

News Corp in December agreed to sell its stake in Australian pay-TV company Foxtel to sports streaming service DAZN Group. News Corp said it intended to focus more sharply on Dow Jones, digital real estate services and book publishing.

More on News Corp's quarterly earnings:

  • Book-publishing revenues grew 8.2% to $595 million. Top titles for HarperCollins included "Cher: The Memoir" and "Wicked" by Gregory Maguire.
  • The Journal said digital subscriptions fell by less than 1% from the September quarter, to an average of 3.79 million. Including the print edition, total subscriptions to the Journal averaged 4.23 million, down slightly from the prior quarter.
  • Dow Jones revenue rose 2.7% from the year-ago quarter and earnings grew 6.7%. Advertising revenue for the segment declined 4%, reflecting a decrease in print advertising. Digital advertising accounted for 64% of total advertising revenue in the quarter.
  • Revenue in the company's digital real estate services segment increased 12.9% to $473 million.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Ford CEO: Tariffs Could Wipe Out Billions in Profits, Hurt Jobs — Market Talk
data.description: Ford CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts that President Trump's proposed tariffs could hurt the U.S. auto industry. "There's no question that tariffs at the 25% level from Canada and Mexico, if they're protracted, would have a huge impact on our industry, with billions of dollars of industry profits wiped out and adverse effects on U.S. jobs," he says. Consumers would also face higher prices, he adds. Ford applauds Trump's administration for the agreements with Mexico and Canada earlier this week and says it is closely monitoring the situation in China. Shares fall 5.3%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: BriaCell Therapeutics Corp. Announces Results of Shareholder Meeting
data.description:
PHILADELPHIA, Pa. and VANCOUVER, British Columbia, Feb. 05, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — BriaCell Therapeutics Corp. (Nasdaq: BCTX, BCTXW) (“BriaCell” or the “Company”), a clinical-stage biotechnology company that develops novel immunotherapies to transform cancer care, is pleased to announce the results of its annual general meeting of shareholders of the Company (the “Shareholders”) for the year ended July 31, 2024 held on February 5, 2025 (the “Meeting”). A total of 36.26% of the Company’s issued and outstanding common shares (the “Common Shares”) were voted at the Meeting.

At the Meeting, the Shareholders overwhelmingly voted in favour of all proposed resolutions, consisting of the re-appointment of MNP LLP as auditors of the Company for the ensuing year and authorizing the directors to fix their remuneration, and the election of each of Dr. William V. Williams, Mr. Jamieson Bondarenko, Dr. Jane A. Gross, Dr. Rebecca Taub, Mr. Vaughn C. Embro-Pantalony, and Mr. Martin E. Schmieg as directors of the Company.

Detailed results of the votes in connection with the election of the directors are set out below:

Director% of Votes For% of Votes Withheld
Dr. William V. Williams88.42%11.58%
Mr. Jamieson Bondarenko86.57%13.43%
Dr. Jane A. Gross88.37%11.63%
Dr. Rebecca Taub88.44%11.56%
Mr. Vaughn C. Embro-Pantalony88.05%11.95%
Mr. Martin E. Schmieg87.99%12.01%

The formal report on voting results with respect to all matters voted upon during the Meeting will be filed on the Company’s SEDAR+ profile at www.sedarplus.ca.

About BriaCell Therapeutics Corp.

BriaCell is a clinical-stage biotechnology company that develops novel immunotherapies to transform cancer care. More information is available at https://briacell.com/.

data.title: Tokyu Corp. Buys Back 5.9 Million Shares
data.description:
Tokyu Corp. repurchased 5.9 million shares for a total of 10.01 billion yen in January, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The company's ongoing buyback program, which started in November 2024, allows for the acquisition of up to 24 million shares for as much as 40 billion yen.

data.title: Zeta Global Accelerates Timing of Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2024 Financial Results; New Date Now February 25, 2025
data.description:
Zeta Global Accelerates Timing of Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2024 Financial Results; New Date Now February 25, 2025

NEW YORK--(BUSINESS WIRE)--February 05, 2025--

Zeta Global , the AI-Powered Marketing Cloud, today announced it has accelerated the release of its fourth quarter and full year 2024 financial results to after the market close on Tuesday, February 25, 2025. The decision to move the review of financial results and business outlook a day earlier was made to avoid conflict with other earnings calls. On this call, Zeta will also share its next long-term model, Zeta 2028, including the KPIs and growth catalysts underlying the plan. Co-Founder, Chairman, and CEO David A. Steinberg and CFO Chris Greiner will host a conference call later that day, at 4:30 p.m. Eastern Time.

A live webcast and replay of the conference call will be accessible on Zeta's Investor Relations website at http://investors.zetaglobal.com.

About Zeta

Zeta Global is the AI-Powered Marketing Cloud that leverages advanced artificial intelligence (AI) and trillions of consumer signals to make it easier for marketers to acquire, grow, and retain customers more efficiently. Through the Zeta Marketing Platform (ZMP), our vision is to make sophisticated marketing simple by unifying identity, intelligence, and omnichannel activation into a single platform — powered by one of the industry's largest proprietary databases and AI. Our enterprise customers across multiple verticals are empowered to personalize experiences with consumers at an individual level across every channel, delivering better results for marketing programs. Zeta was founded in 2007 by David A. Steinberg and John Sculley and is headquartered in New York City with offices around the world. To learn more, go to www.zetaglobal.com.

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250205185935/en/

CONTACT: Investor Relations

Matt Pfau

ir@zetaglobal.com

Press

Candace Dean

press@zetaglobal.com

data.title: Coherent Shares Rise After 2Q Earnings Beat
data.description:
By Stephen Nakrosis

Shares of Coherent climbed after the company reported second-quarter financial results which bested some analysts' estimates.

The stock was up 12%, at $101.43, after hours Wednesday. The stock closed the day's regular session with a gain of 2.6%, finishing at $90.10.

Coherent reported second-quarter earnings per share of 44 cents on revenue of $1.43 billion. Analysts were expecting EPS of 1 cent on revenue of $1.374 billion, according to FactSet.

On an adjusted basis, the company reported second-quarter earnings of 95 cents a share. Analysts were expecting adjusted earnings per share of 69 cents, FactSet said.

In the year-ago period, the company reported a loss per share of 38 cents on revenue of $1.13 billion. The company said it was expecting adjusted EPS of 75 cents to 95 cents in the third quarter, and revenue of $1.39 billion to $1.48 billion.

Coherent makes and sells engineered materials, opto-electronic components and lasers.

Write to Stephen Nakrosis at stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com

data.title: Centuria Office REIT's Result Points to Trough in Asset Valuations — Market Talk
data.description: Centuria Office REIT's 1H result suggests to Bell Potter that asset valuations are nearing a trough. It highlights that valuations of 37% of Centuria Office REIT's portfolio increased or stabilized in 1H. "This, along with improving office transaction volumes in 1H, suggest book values are close to 'the bottom'," says analyst Connor Eldridge. Bell Potter now estimates an expansion of 40 basis points in Centuria Office REIT's 1H weighted average capitalization rate over the next 12 months. Evidence that asset values are finding a floor "will alleviate balance-sheet pressure," Bell Potter says. It notes that Centuria Office REIT had gearing of 42.9% at the end of June, above its 25%-35% target range. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: FP Partner Opens Second Money Doctor Premier in Nara, Japan
data.description:
FP Partner launched its second Money Doctor Premier store in Japan's Nara City, located at AEON Mall Kashihara, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

This marks the 30th store in the network, offering free, personalized financial consultations.

The company plans to expand to 50 locations by November 2026. The reopening of AEON Mall Kashihara is expected to boost customer traffic and raise awareness of Money Doctor services.
